unit_name: Sampling Distributions and the Central Limit Theorem
hook: You already know how to calculate the mean and standard deviation of a dataset — but what happens when you take many different random samples from the same population? Each sample gives a slightly different answer, and that variability has a hidden, beautiful structure you haven't seen yet. This unit reveals why statisticians can confidently draw conclusions about millions of people from just a few hundred observations.
concept: The unifying idea is that the behavior of sample statistics (like the sample mean $\bar{x}$) is itself predictable and well-behaved, even when individual data points are not — a principle formalized as the concept of a *sampling distribution*.

topic_roadmap.description: You will learn to describe and interpret the distribution of a sample statistic across many repeated samples, distinguishing it clearly from the distribution of the original data.

Master the Central Limit Theorem to explain why the sampling distribution of $\bar{x}$ approaches a normal distribution with mean $\mu$ and standard deviation $\frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$ as sample size grows.

topic_roadmap.topic_name: Simulation and the Law of Large Numbers
topic_roadmap.description: Apply simulation techniques to see the Law of Large Numbers in action, building intuition for why sample estimates reliably converge to true population values as $n$ increases.
You will learn to use the normal distribution as a practical approximation for binomial probabilities when $n$ is large, connecting the Central Limit Theorem directly to counting problems.
